params.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup appears. Shows student's data with all four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10). Each scale displays a checkmark indicating it worked for that data.

beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!

elements.description: Scale Preview System showing student's warmup data with all four scales marked as working.

beats.text: But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System showing student's warmup data with all four scales marked as working.

params.description: Data table appears showing Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read This Month. Axis shows 0 to 50 with scale of 5. Graph is currently empty.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most: 80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph. Books Read This Month. Axis 0 to 50, scale of 5. Empty graph.

beats.text: Let me try Scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars draw normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the top boundary at 50. Warning indicator appears.
Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator visible.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars visible. Dana's bar extends past axis top at 50. Warning indicator visible.

beats.text: I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see. I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80.

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear sequentially: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now shows 0 to 80.
Axis extended to 80. All tick marks visible: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Dana's bar now fits within the graph.
Axis extension complete. Dana's bar (80) now fully visible within graph boundary.

beats.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 80, scale of 5. All 17 tick marks visible and highlighted. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55), Dana (80) bars fully visible.

beats.text: It works, but is there a better way?
When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 80, scale of 5. All 17 tick marks visible. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55), Dana (80) bars fully visible.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. The guide said to try Scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 button highlights.

steps.text: Here's what to do: click the button that says Scale of 10. That's the one we're testing now.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 10 button. Graph updates to scale of 10 showing 9 tick marks.

steps.text: Let me show you. I click Scale of 10, right here. The graph changes to show tick marks at 0, 10, 20, all the way to 80. That's 9 marks total, much fewer than the 17 we had with Scale of 5. The answer is Scale of 10 because it makes the graph cleaner and easier to read.

incorrects.description: Student selected 20: confused smallest with biggest

params.description: Values 20 and 80 both highlight for comparison.

steps.text: 20 is the smallest number. We need the biggest. Which value is larger, 20 or 80?

incorrects.description: Student selected 35: middle value confusion

params.description: Values 35, 55, and 80 highlight to show comparison.

steps.text: 35 is one of the middle values. Look at all four numbers. Which one is greatest?

incorrects.description: Student selected 55: close but not the largest

params.description: Values 55 and 80 both highlight for direct comparison.

steps.text: 55 is close, but there's one value bigger. Compare 55 and 80. Which is greater?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Value 80 highlights and pulses to indicate it is the maximum.

steps.text: Let me show you. I look at each number: 20, 35, 55, 80. I compare them. 80 is greater than all the others. The biggest number is 80. That is what we check first when choosing a scale.

incorrects.description: Student has not yet clicked all four scale buttons on first attempt

steps.text: Keep going. Try all the scale buttons to see which ones work.

incorrects.description: Student has not yet clicked all four scale buttons on second attempt

params.description: All four scale buttons highlight.

steps.text: Click each of the four scale buttons. We need to check if 23 fits on each one.

incorrects.description: Student has not clicked all four scale buttons after three attempts. System models the exploration.

params.description: System demonstrates clicking each scale button in sequence: 1, 2, 5, 10. Each displays briefly with checkmark.

steps.text: Let me show you. I click each scale button to see if 23 fits. Scale of 1 works. Scale of 2 works. Scale of 5 works. Scale of 10 works. All four scales can show 23. That's the answer.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Which scale shows every single number from 0 to 23?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 1 button highlights.

steps.text: Think about which scale counts by 1. That scale will have a mark for every number. Which button is that?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 after three attempts. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 1 button. Graph updates to show scale of 1 with 24 tick marks.

steps.text: Here's how to think about it. Scale of 1 counts by 1, so every whole number from 0 to 23 gets its own tick mark. That's 24 marks total. The answer is Scale of 1.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. We want to compare Scale of 10 to Scale of 1.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 button highlights.

steps.text: Let's see what happens with Scale of 10. Look for the button that shows 10.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System demonstrates the correct action.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 10 button. Graph updates to show scale of 10 with axis marks at 0, 10, 20, 30.

steps.text: I'll click Scale of 10 for us. Now you can see the axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. That's what we wanted to compare.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the ones place digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. Which scale shows any whole number exactly?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Values 7, 19, 23 highlight in data table.

steps.text: Check 7, 19, and 23. They don't end in 0 or 5, so they aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 lets you place any whole number exactly. Which button shows Scale of 2?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale button three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 2. System demonstrates how each bar lands exactly on a tick mark or halfway between: 7 is halfway between 6 and 8, 12 is on the line at 12, 19 is halfway between 18 and 20, 23 is halfway between 22 and 24.

steps.text: Let me show you. Scale of 2 works because half of 2 is 1. That means every whole number lands exactly on a line or halfway between two lines. Look: 7 lands halfway between 6 and 8, 12 is right on 12, 19 is halfway between 18 and 20, and 23 is halfway between 22 and 24. The correct answer is Scale of 2, the scale that shows any whole number exactly.

incorrects.description: Student clicked Data Set A on first attempt

steps.text: Check those ones place digits again. Do they include digits that aren't 0 or 5?

incorrects.description: Student clicked Data Set A on second attempt

params.description: Ones place digits 7, 2, 9, 3 highlight on Data Set B panel.

steps.text: Let's look at Data Set B. The ones digits are 7, 2, 9, 3. Notice none of those are 0 or 5. Which set has that pattern?

incorrects.description: Student clicked Data Set A three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Data Set B panel highlights. Ones place digits 7, 2, 9, 3 pulse in sequence.

steps.text: Data Set B is the answer. Look at the ones place: 7, 2, 9, 3. None of those digits are 0 or 5, so these numbers aren't multiples of 5. That's the signal that Scale of 2 will show all values exactly. When you see ones digits that aren't 0 or 5, think Scale of 2.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1

steps.text: Scale of 1 shows exact values, but count how many marks you'd need to reach 31.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Look at 22, 8, and 31. Do they end in 0 or 5? Those numbers won't land exactly on a line with Scale of 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Check the ones place: 2, 5, 8, 1. Only 15 ends in 0 or 5. Scale of 10 won't show the others exactly.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The ones place digits 2, 5, 8, 1 highlight sequentially in the data table.

steps.text: Let me show you. Check each ones place digit: 2, 5, 8, 1. Three of them aren't 0 or 5, so they aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 lets us show any whole number exactly because half of 2 is 1. The answer is Scale of 2.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: Scale of 2 and Scale of 10 highlighted for comparison.

steps.text: Scale of 2 works, but look at how many marks that takes to reach 60. Count by 2s: that's 30 tick marks! Scale of 10 gives us the same information with only 6 marks.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 highlighted for comparison.

steps.text: Scale of 5 fits the data and shows exact values. But for numbers up to 60, Scale of 10 keeps the axis cleaner. Count: Scale of 5 needs 12 marks, Scale of 10 needs only 6.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 highlights with checkmark.

steps.text: Let me show you. The biggest number is 60. All the values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 shows every value exactly. Count the marks: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. Only 6 marks. That's what makes it easiest to read. The answer is Scale of 10.

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect options, no correct answers

params.description: All four values highlight in data table.

steps.text: Let's go back to what makes a good scale. Look at the values: 60, 45, 30, 15. Check the ones place digits. What pattern do you see?

or.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected some correct reasons but not all

params.description: Scale Preview System highlights all three scales with comparison indicators.

steps.text: You found some reasons. There are more. Think about the ones place digits AND how many marks each scale needs to reach 60.

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

or.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.
The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect options

params.description: Ones place digits (0, 5, 0, 5) pulse in data table.

steps.text: Some of your choices are right. But check which statements are actually true. Look at the ones digits and count how many marks each scale needs.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale Preview System animates comparison showing tick mark counts for each scale reaching 60.

steps.text: Here's why all four are true. First: 60, 45, 30, 15 all end in 0 or 5. That means Scale of 10 shows them precisely. Second: fewer marks means easier to read. Third: 60 fits on all three scales shown. Fourth: Scale of 2 needs 30 marks to reach 60, but Scale of 10 needs only 6. All four reasons support choosing Scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Scale of 1 would need 41 tick marks to reach the biggest number. That's a lot of lines. Look at the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. Which scale shows any whole number exactly without too many marks?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Look at the ones place: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5. Scale of 5 won't land exactly for these numbers. Which scale shows any whole number clearly?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Check the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. None end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 won't show exact values. Which scale works for any whole number?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Ones place digits (7, 4, 1, 3) highlight in sequence.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, I check the ones place digits: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 works for any whole number. Half of 2 is 1, so every number lands exactly on a line or halfway between. The answer is Scale of 2.

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the data table. Count up by 2s from zero to match each value.

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Gridlines at each tick mark highlight to show counting points.

steps.text: Here's what helps: each line on the axis goes up by 2. Find your value in the table, then count by 2s on the graph. Guitar is 27, so count 0, 2, 4, 6... up to 26, then one more to the halfway mark.

incorrects.description: Student set bar heights incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing each bar in sequence: Piano at 14, Guitar at 27 (halfway between 26 and 28), Drums at 33 (halfway between 32 and 34), Violin at 41 (halfway between 40 and 42).

steps.text: Let me walk you through this. Piano is 14, right on the line. Guitar is 27, halfway between 26 and 28. Drums is 33, halfway between 32 and 34. Violin is 41, halfway between 40 and 42. With Scale of 2, odd numbers land halfway and even numbers land exactly on lines. That's how the bar graph shows Minutes of Practice correctly.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: Yellow Team row highlights showing value 70.

steps.text: Scale of 1 would need 70 tick marks to reach the biggest number. That's too many lines on the axis. Think bigger.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: All values highlight. Ones place digits visible: 5, 5, 0, 0.

steps.text: Look at the last digit in each number. 25, 55, 70, 40. All end in 0 or 5. That tells you a bigger scale will work.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Yellow Team row highlights showing value 70.

steps.text: Scale of 5 would work, but you'd need 14 tick marks from 0 to 70. There's a cleaner choice.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights ones place digits in sequence: 5, 5, 0, 0. All end in 0 or 5.

steps.text: Let me show you. Check the ones place: 25, 55, 70, 40. All end in 0 or 5, so Scales of 5 and 10 both work. The biggest number is 70. Scale of 5 needs 14 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 7. Scale of 10 is the cleanest choice because it shows all values exactly with the fewest marks on the axis.

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the data table. How many points did Red Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar to wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Red Team row highlights showing value 25.

steps.text: Red Team scored 25 points. On this scale, 25 is halfway between 20 and 30. Where does that bar need to end?

incorrects.description: Student set Red Team bar incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Red Team bar placement. Bar extends from Red Team category to 25, landing halfway between tick marks at 20 and 30.

steps.text: Let me show you. Red Team scored 25 points. I find 20 on the axis, then 30. 25 is halfway between those two marks. The bar ends exactly there. That's where 25 lands on a scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Blue Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar to wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Blue Team row highlights showing value 40.

steps.text: Blue Team scored 40 points. Find 40 on the axis. Where does that bar need to end?

incorrects.description: Student set Blue Team bar incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Blue Team bar placement. Bar extends from Blue Team category to 40, landing exactly on the 40 tick mark.

steps.text: Let me show you. Blue Team scored 40 points. I find 40 on the axis. It's right on a tick mark. The bar ends exactly there. That's 40 on a scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student set Green Team bar to wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Green Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Green Team bar to wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Green Team row highlights showing value 55.

steps.text: Green Team scored 55 points. On this scale, 55 is halfway between 50 and 60. Where does that bar need to end?

incorrects.description: Student set Green Team bar incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Green Team bar placement. Bar extends from Green Team category to 55, landing halfway between tick marks at 50 and 60.

steps.text: Let me show you. Green Team scored 55 points. I find 50 on the axis, then 60. 55 is halfway between those two marks. The bar ends exactly there. That's where 55 lands on a scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to wrong height on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many points did Yellow Team score?

incorrects.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Yellow Team row highlights showing value 70.

steps.text: Yellow Team scored 70 points. Find 70 on the axis. Where does that bar need to end?

incorrects.description: Student set Yellow Team bar incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Yellow Team bar placement. Bar extends from Yellow Team category to 70, landing exactly on the 70 tick mark.

steps.text: Let me show you. Yellow Team scored 70 points. I find 70 on the axis. It's right on a tick mark. The bar ends exactly there. That's 70 on a scale of 10.

params.description: Vertical data table displays Team Scores with categories Red Team, Green Team, Yellow Team, Blue Team and values 25, 55, 70, 40.
Horizontal bar graph showing Team Scores. Red Team: 25, Green Team: 55, Yellow Team: 70, Blue Team: 40. Scale of 10, axis range 0–80.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number. Does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits. Are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Team Scores: Red Team 25, Green Team 55, Yellow Team 70, Blue Team 40.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Team Scores data. Scale of 10, axis range 0–80.

params.description: Data table fades from screen.
Bar graph fades from screen.
